sources.source_title: 15th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution: Voting Rights (1870)
sources.notes: National Archives milestone document page explaining that the 15th Amendment granted African American men the right to vote and marked a major constitutional expansion of political rights after the Civil War.

sources.source_title: Introduction To Federal Voting Rights Laws
sources.notes: DOJ overview explaining that the 15th Amendment prohibited denial or abridgment of the right to vote on the basis of race, color, or previous condition of servitude.

sources.source_title: Laws and Court Cases: Black Americans and the Vote
sources.notes: National Archives reference page placing the 15th Amendment within the longer history of Black voting rights and later legal protections.
Senate history page explaining that Congress passed the Enforcement Acts to combat violence and intimidation aimed at suppressing Black voting and political participation during Reconstruction.
U.S. Senate page hosting the original document image for the Enforcement Act of 1870.

sources.source_title: Introduction To Federal Voting Rights Laws
sources.notes: DOJ overview stating that Congress enacted the Enforcement Act of 1870 after ratification of the 15th Amendment, including criminal penalties for interference with the right to vote.
Senate history page describing the 1871 Enforcement Acts, including the expanded federal power used to combat Ku Klux Klan terror and protect African American civil and voting rights.
U.S. Senate page hosting the document image for the April 1871 Enforcement Act, commonly associated with the Ku Klux Klan Act.

sources.source_title: Commander-in-Chief: U.S. Presidents and their Executive Power
sources.notes: National Archives blog noting that President Grant signed the Ku Klux Klan Act of 1871 as part of efforts to protect African Americans from organized racial terror during Reconstruction.
DOJ Civil Rights Division page stating that the Division was created in 1957 by enactment of the Civil Rights Act of 1957.

sources.source_title: Organization, Mission and Functions Manual: Civil Rights Division
sources.notes: DOJ organizational history explaining that the Civil Rights Act of 1957 created the Civil Rights Division and the Office of the Assistant Attorney General for Civil Rights.

sources.source_title: The Civil Rights Division Celebrates 60th Anniversary
sources.notes: DOJ anniversary statement describing the 1957 Act as the first civil rights law passed since Reconstruction and an early step toward later major civil rights statutes.

sources.source_title: Title 52 - Voting and Elections - Subtitle I and II
sources.notes: DOJ statutory page showing the voting and election provisions codified from federal voting-rights laws, including the Civil Rights Act of 1960.

sources.source_title: Federal Law Constraints on Post-Election Audits
sources.notes: DOJ guidance explaining that the Civil Rights Act of 1960 requires retention and preservation of election records for 22 months after certain federal elections.

sources.source_title: Papers of Clarence Mitchell Jr. and of the NAACP
sources.notes: National Archives project description noting Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s role in helping secure passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1960 and other major civil rights legislation.

sources.source_title: Laws and Court Cases: Black Americans and the Vote
sources.notes: National Archives page explaining that the 24th Amendment prohibited poll taxes in federal elections and notes that poll taxes had long been used to disenfranchise poor voters and people of color.
National Archives voting-rights overview identifying the 24th Amendment as part of the constitutional and statutory chain of voting-rights protections.

sources.source_title: Legal Provisions Relevant to the Electoral College Process
sources.notes: National Archives page reproducing the text of the 24th Amendment and its prohibition on denial or abridgment of the vote because of failure to pay a poll tax or other tax in federal elections.
